EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system slid down the coast and stayed offshore, pulling cold air from east of the Cascades into western Oregon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports on Facebook of 8.0 inches of snow in Sandy and Estacada.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system caused very windy conditions, with some locally strong winds, through the Columbia River Gorge, across the Columbia Basin and the Kittitas valley. Localized blowing dust also occurred in the Basin and in the Grande Ronde valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a dust storm in the Grande Ronde valley posted on the NWS Pendleton Facebook page. Dust storm reduced visibility in the Cove area to 500 yards or less. Report received at 715 PM PDT (1815 LST). Blowing dust also occurred at and around the La Grande airport where sustained winds of 29-39 mph and gusts of 35-47 mph occurred between 545 and 700 PM (1645 and 1800 LST).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire broke out on the Bear Creek Greenway near Penninger Road and Pine Street in Central Point late in the afternoon of 07/17/18.  The point of origin was near a transient camp.  The fire spread quickly to the south and east through fields surrounded by structures. At its peak, it burned into lands bordering the Rogue Valley International Airport.  A large area was evacuated, and the NWS personnel in Medford were forced to abandon the NWS station when the fire approached within 100 feet of the station building.  The fire was controlled in the evening after 97 acres burned. Five outbuildings were destroyed; three residences and one additional outbuilding were damaged. One male died in the fire. His body was discovered in the greenway the following day.

His body was discovered in the greenway the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the region led to a stretch of hot day July 12 through July 17th. Hot temperatures led people to cool off in local rivers. There were two drownings recorded on July 16 and July 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures on July 16th near the Sandy River in Troutdale got up to 98 degrees, the day a swimmer went underwater in the Sandy River near Glen Otto Community Park. He later died in the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the region led to a stretch of hot day July 12 through July 17th. Hot temperatures led people to cool off in local rivers. There were two drownings recorded on July 16 and July 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man swimming across the Willamette River from Yamhill County to Marion County near the Wheatland Ferry drowned. It had been hot the previous several days, and though temperatures were a bit cooler on the 18th with highs only in the upper 80s, it's believed that the heat over the previous several days led him to swim in the river that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Boxcar Fire was reported on June 21, 07:48 a.m. after a storm brought patchy rain and lightning to portions of Central and North-Central Oregon. This fire eventually merged with the South Junction Fire which started around 3:00 p.m. the same day. These fires burned southeast and south of Maupin in Wasco county Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Jack Knife Fire was reported on June 22, 08:11 a.m. after a storm brought patchy rain and lightning to North-Central Oregon. The fire was burning in heavy sage brush and grass, with scattered juniper. The fire area is located in remote and rugged terrain within the Lower John Day Wilderness Study Area in Sherman county Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This fire burned near Warm Springs in Jefferson county Oregon in early July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire that started July 2, in the area of the old Warm Springs mill, threatened homes and caused the closure of U.S. Highway 26 for about six hours before crews gained control of the wind-whipped fire.  The Mecca Fire was estimated at about 800 acres on Tuesday morning, July 3, when Warm Springs transferred command of the fire to a Central Oregon Type 3 fire management team.  A Level 3 evacuation notice, which means Go, had been issued for the Greeley Heights area along Jefferson Street, all the way to the water tower road. Other areas of Greeley Heights were under a Level 2 notice ��� meaning they should Be Set to go.  Greeley Heights returned to a Level 1 evacuation notice, which means to Be Ready by Tuesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Substation Fire started on private land southeast of The Dalles, Oregon, on Tuesday, July 17, 2018 and grew quickly. Driven by strong winds, the fire moved more than 18 miles in the first burning period and crossed multiple agency jurisdictions. The fire covered an area that extended from 5 miles south of The Dalles, to 2 miles west of Moro, to the  limits of Grass Valley, in both Wasco and Sherman counties in Oregon. One person died while operating a tractor cutting a fire line. Also a historic homestead was lost. Fire is being investigated as possible arson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

The fire was first reported Thursday July 26th, and it quickly spread that afternoon and evening and into the Deschutes River canyon. Firefighters coordinated the fire alongside Wasco and Sherman Counties and local rangeland fire protection association firefighters. This was Wasco and Sherman County���s third major fire this season.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain in and around Cove, in Union county.  One half inch to one inch of rain fell on this area over approximately a one and half hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain over portions of southern Deschutes county.  Small hail also occurred with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms caused heavy rain in and around La Pine, in Deschutes county.   Rain fall of up to 1.3 inches occurred with ponding of water on driveways and property due to poor drainage of relatively flat ground.  Small hail occurred, piling to a depth of 1 inch, 4 miles SW of La Pine causing standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent moist southwest flow continued into the 25th of May.  Thunderstorms developed late morning and continued well into the evening.  The storms produced locally heavy rain and some flooding.  Also some hail occurred with the storms.  The hail was mainly small, but did reach to 1 inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Largest hail estimated at 1 inch in diameter near Imnaha in Wallowa county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong stationary low pressure system off the British Columbia coast brought impressively high seas into the Oregon Coast.  Wave heights up to 37 feet were recorded at buoys off the coast, with top one-tenth wave heights up to 45 feet. Damaging surf caused severe beach erosion, damaged a couple buildings right along the beach, injured one person, and killed one person.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clatsop County emergency manager reported severe beach erosion and damage to trails near the Peter Iredale Shipwreck, northward to south Jetty. About 5 to 6 feet of dune entirely eroded and swept out to sea. Tillamook Emergency manager reported Seaside and Cannon Beach had water in streets. Logs and other debris washed up on closed roads. In Neskowin, a hotel prepared ahead of time and had minimal loss from flooding of bottom floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong stationary low pressure system off the British Columbia coast brought impressively high seas into the Oregon Coast.  Wave heights up to 37 feet were recorded at buoys off the coast, with top one-tenth wave heights up to 45 feet. Damaging surf caused severe beach erosion, damaged a couple buildings right along the beach, injured one person, and killed one person.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was swept off rocks and into the sea in Depoe Bay and died. One woman was injured walking along the beach in Lincoln City when she was hit by a wave carrying a large log.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough progressing over the area brought showers and thunderstorms to SW Washington and NW Oregon. Heavy rain was reported in downtown Portland, with multiple reports and pictures showing streets flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water reported near NE 28th Ave between Glisan and Burnside. Trimet MAX lines were temporarily closed due to standing water between Lloyd Center and Hollywood.  Rain guage at the Portland Airport reported 0.82 of an inch of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple and their young daughter took a trail down to rocks which overlook the ocean. A large wave washed over the rocks and swept the couple off the rocks and into the ocean. Both were taken to a local hospital where they were pronounced dead. Significant wave heights were around 5 feet at the time of the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple and their young daughter took a trail down to rocks which overlook the ocean. A large wave washed over the rocks and swept the couple off the rocks and into the ocean. Both were taken to a local hospital where they were pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable atmosphere with plenty of sun breaks allowed updrafts to develop. One such updraft developed into a dust devil which did some damage to a storage building at the Creswell Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil developed, hitting a storage building at the Creswell Airport. The dust devil peeled back a 20-foot section of the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The South Umpqua Complex consisted of at least seven major fires, all of which were caused by lightning. The time of ignition was around 1000 PDT on 07/15/2018. As of 0330 PDT on 08/07/18, the fire covered 28723 acres and was 18 percent contained. 1 structure was lost and 26.0 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts. Later in the day on 08/17/18, fire management was reorganized and the South Umpqua Complex ceased to exist as an accountable entity.  Fire Management was assigned to other units.  The Columbus, Snowshoe and Miles fire became stand alone fires for accounting and management efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Sugar Pine Wildfire consisted of at least five wildfires, all of which were caused by lightning. Fires included the Goodview, Union, and Round Top fires. The time of ignition was around 1200 PDT on 07/15/2018.  As of the last report at 0330 PST on 08/03/18, the Goodview fire covered 1795 acres and was 87 percent contained. 3.0 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Substation Fire started on private land southeast of The Dalles, Oregon, on Tuesday, July 17, 2018 and grew quickly. Driven by strong winds, the fire moved more than 18 miles in the first burning period and crossed multiple agency jurisdictions. The fire covered an area that extended from 5 miles south of The Dalles, to 2 miles west of Moro, to the limits of Grass Valley, in both Wasco and Sherman counties in Oregon. One person died while operating a tractor cutting a fire line. Also a historic homestead was lost. Fire is being investigated as possible arson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A number of thunderstorms developed over central Oregon on the afternoon of August 16th.  One of these storms became severe with large hail.  Thunderstorms continued to move into northeast Oregon in the evening, leaving the area around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced hail up to 1 inch in diameter at the Cloverdale Fire Station, 5 miles SE of Sisters in Deschutes county.  Heavy rain also caused flooding of the parking lot associated with the station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 6, 2018, around 2 PM the US Coast Guard noticed a vessel had capsized while crossing the bar at the mouth of the Siuslaw River, killing one person. There was weak high pressure over the area at the time, with winds out of the north gusting to 10 kt. Wave heights were generally around 5 to 6 feet with periods at 10 to 11 seconds, but waves in the bar were breaking.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 2 PM the US Coast Guard noticed a vessel had capsized while crossing the bar at the mouth of the Siuslaw River. The captain was pulled from the Ocean and later pronounced dead at the hospital. Wave heights at the time were generally around 5 to 6 feet with periods at 10 to 11 seconds, but the waves in the bar were breaking.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed over Southwest Baker County, Oregon on June 20th, leading to flash flooding and debris flow on the Rail and Cornet-Windy Ridge fires burn scar areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall moved across parts of Southwest Baker County causing flash flooding and debris flow on burn scars southwest of Unity along the South Fork of the Burnt River, and along Highway 245 from northeast of Unity to around 5 miles south of the intersection with Highway 7. Weather observations from around the area reported up to one and a half inches of rain over a period of about one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Whiskey Fire was reported shortly after 11 a.m. Thursday and broke out about eight miles north of Warm Springs, below the base of the Mutton Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whiskey fire consumed roughly 1,000 acres of grass and scrub brush on the Warm Springs Indian Reservation in southern Wasco county Oregon. Containment lines were holding as of June 23rd. No further information available after that date.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Jack Knife Fire was reported on June 22, 08:11 a.m. after a storm brought patchy rain and lightning to North-Central Oregon. The fire was burning in heavy sage brush and grass, with scattered juniper. The fire area is located in remote and rugged terrain within the Lower John Day Wilderness Study Area in Sherman county Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning-caused Graham Fire was reported early in the afternoon on Thursday, June 21, 2018, burning four miles south of the Metolius River arm of Lake Billy Chinook, near Culver, in Jefferson county Oregon. The fire burned primarily on private land protected by the Oregon Department of Forestry and Lake Chinook Fire and Rescue. Numerous homes were threatened in the Three Rivers Subdivision and evacuations were issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Boxcar Fire was reported on June 21, 07:48 a.m. after a storm brought patchy rain and lightning to portions of Central and North-Central Oregon. This fire eventually merged with the South Junction Fire which started around 3:00 p.m. the same day. These fires burned southeast and south of Maupin in Wasco county Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms in the north Pacific generated heavy swell trains that created high surf along the Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy 46027 near Point St. George recorded swell that would generate heavy surf from 16/1550 PST until 17/1350 PST.  The peak swell was 24 feet at 19 seconds at 16/1950 PST, which would generate wave faces of 35 feet.  Buoy 46015 near Port Orford recorded swell that would generate heavy surf from 16/1550 PST until 17/1650 PST.  The peak swell was 24 feet at 19 seconds at 16/1550 PST, which would generate wave faces of 35 feet. Buoy 46229 went adrift just after the heavy swell moved in, but it still recorded swell that would support heavy surf from 16/1453 PST until 16/1523 PST, when it went adrift. The swell was 19 feet at 20 seconds at 16/1453 PST, which would generate wave faces of 31 feet.  The swell was 20 feet at 17 seconds at 16/1523 PST, which would generate wave faces of 29 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a strong thermal gradient between the lower Treasure Valley and the Columbia Basin and significant momentum transfer of strong winds aloft created localized areas of significant damaging winds in the foothills of the Blue Mountains and Grande Ronde Valley. Interstate 84 was closed for several hours between Pendleton and La Grande after a tractor trailer was blown over coming off the Blue Mountains toward Pendleton. A mesonet station in close proximity to this blow over reported a gust of 78 knots (90 mph) around the time of the accident. Portions of Highway 11 between Pendleton and Milton-Freewater were also closed briefly as a precaution with crosswind gusts estimated at 61 knots (70 mph). Winds decreased rapidly away from the foot of the mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell nearly hitting a residence in Redmond.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Long Prairie RAWS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 16/0813 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which moved through on October 27th brought a much cooler air mass in aloft, which destabilized the atmosphere enough for thunderstorm development across the area on the 28th and 29th. There was enough vertical shear in the environment for a few of theses vigorous showers and thunderstorms to start to rotate, three of which went on to produce tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was damage to multiple small shrubby trees just north of the railroad track along Libby Ln. There was also a sign knocked over at the NW corner of Libby Ln and Jefferson-Marion Rd, but whether or not it was caused by a tornado could not be determined. The tornado was likely on the ground for one minute or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Miles Wildfire was originally part of the South Umpqua Complex Wildfire.  It was caused by lightning and the time of ignition was around 1200 PDT on 07/15/2018. It was separated from the South Umpqua Complex on 08/08/18 and combined with the remaining  fires from the former Sugar Pine Complex.  As of the last report at 0330 PST on 10/03/18, the fire covered 54334 acres and was 82 percent contained. 2 structures were lost, and 95.8 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

As of the last report at 0330 PST on 10/03/18, the fire covered 54334 acres and was 82 percent contained. 2 structures were lost, and 95.8 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist upslope flow into the Blue Mountains produced heavy rain with rainfall rates of up to one inch per hour and storm total accumulations between one and three inches. Localized flooding was reported near the town of Heppner where water inside a residence forced an evacuation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of water inside a residence due to heavy rain forced four adults and a child to leave their house on the 54200 Block of Highway 74 in Heppner.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tree damage was observed several locations, including at the Portland Meadows facility, on MLK Boulevard near Union Court, and at a park along NE Bridgeton Road. Three semi-truck trailers were overturned in a parking area near Marine Drive. Minor roof damage also was noted to a building southwest of the Marine Drive and 2nd Avenue intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a low to the north and another low to the south, there were several showers across northwest Oregon. There was enough shear for one stronger shower to spin up a brief funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A viewer sent in a photo to a local news station of a funnel cloud over the Multnomah Channel, observed around 11 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Klondike consisted of the Klondike and Granite fires, which merged into one fire. Both fires were caused by lightning. The time of ignition was around 1830 PDT on 07/15/2018. As of 0700 PDT on 11/02/18, the fire covered 175258 acres and was 80 percent contained. 0 structures were lost and 95.8 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple greenhouse structures were damaged at a farm on Thatcher Road. Witness accounts also confirmed a tornado. The tornado was likely on the ground for about 30 seconds to 1 minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Stukel fire started on the afternoon of 10/14/2018, and it was caused by a shooter using exploding targets.  As of the last report at 0700 PST on 10/23/2018, the fire covered 518 acres and was 100% contained.  0 structures were lost and 875,000 dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies combined with a cool and very dry air mass to bring freezing temperatures to parts of southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 21 to 51 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies combined with a cool and very dry air mass to bring freezing temperatures to parts of southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 32 to 37 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 39 year old man was found deceased in an irrigation ditch in Medford on the morning of 11/12/2018.  Evidence collected indicated that hypothermia was the cause of death.  The low temperature in Medford that morning was 25 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 39 year old man was found deceased in an irrigation ditch in Medford on the morning of 11/12/2018.  Evidence collected indicated that hypothermia was the cause of death.  The low temperature in Medford that morning was 25 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high wind gusts to a station in south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 21/1138 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another front brought high winds to a few locations along the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flynn Prairie RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 27/0013 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved onto the coast, bringing high winds, mainly to beaches and headlands along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oregon DOT weather sensor measured gusts up to 78 mph on Megler Bridge. Also, in Pacific City, a weather station measured wind gusts up to 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved onto the coast, bringing high winds, mainly to beaches and headlands along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lincoln County Emergency Manager reported a home was damaged in Lincoln City.  They also lost a microwave dish on top of Otter Crest tower.  A weather station in Lincoln City measured wind gusts up to 58 mph.  A weather station on Gleneden Beach measured wind gusts up to 67 mph. Another weather station on the Yaquina Bridge measured wind gusts up to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to portions of southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Port Orford NOS/NWLON reported gusts exceeding 58 mph continuously during this interval.  The peak gust was 69 mph recorded at 22/1148 PST.  The Long Prairie RAWS recorded a gust to 63 mph at 22/1113 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to portions of southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flynn Prairie RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval, but mostly on the morning of the 22nd.  The peak gust was 85 mph recorded at 22/1113 PST. The Gold Beach ASOS recorded gusts to 60 mph at 22/1059 PST and 67 mph at 22/1159 PST.  The ODOT sensor at Gold Beach recorded a gust to 69 mph at 22/1216 PST.  The Red Mound RAWS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 22/1221 PST. A tree was reported down on Highway 101 just south of Pistol River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to portions of southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS reported gusts exceeding 57 mph nearly continuously during this interval.  The peak gust was 69 mph recorded at 23/1538 PST.  The Summit RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 22/1803 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked northeast into British Columbia.  The associated cold front brought with it strong southerly winds on the north and central Oregon coast.  This system also brought windy conditions to the Willamette Valley, bringing down tree limbs and a few trees, which caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations on Megler Bridge and Clatsop Spit both observed sustained winds up to 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked northeast into British Columbia.  The associated cold front brought with it strong southerly winds on the north and central Oregon coast.  This system also brought windy conditions to the Willamette Valley, bringing down tree limbs and a few trees, which caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Yaquina Bridge recorded wind gusts up to 71 mph.  A Florence C-MAN station also recorded sustained winds up to 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked northeast into British Columbia.  The associated cold front brought with it strong southerly winds on the north and central Oregon coast.  This system also brought windy conditions to the Willamette Valley, bringing down tree limbs and a few trees, which caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations across the Portland metro generally reported wind gusts up to 35 to 45 mph.  One weather station at Jantzen Beach reported wind gusts up to 52 mph. At least 4900 people were without power, according to PGE and Pacific Power due to downed limbs and a few downed trees on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked northeast into British Columbia.  The associated cold front brought with it strong southerly winds on the north and central Oregon coast.  This system also brought windy conditions to the Willamette Valley, bringing down tree limbs and a few trees, which caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations across the southern Willamette Valley recorded wind gusts up to 35 to 45 mph.  The strongest was at the Eugene Airport, which recorded wind gusts up to 45 mph.  Winds caused a tree to fall in Eugene, barely missing a woman who had just moved her new car out of the driveway where the tree fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Alaska brought a strong cold front through.  This generated strong winds across northwest Oregon, and also brought heavy rain which caused flooding on the Tillamook river.  Large seas also caused damage in spots along beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Megler Bridge recorded wind gusts up to 65 mph.  Winds also caused power outages impacting 209 customers in Warrenton, according to Pacific Power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast toward Victoria Island.  The trailing cold front moved onto the coast, bringing strong southerly winds ahead of the front to the coast and the coast range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 4,000 lost power according to the Tillamook Emergency Manager. A weather station on Megler Bridge recorded wind gusts up to 75 mph.  Other weather stations recorded wind gusts up to 60 to 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast toward Victoria Island.  The trailing cold front moved onto the coast, bringing strong southerly winds ahead of the front to the coast and the coast range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations on the Yaquina Bridge and at Gleneden Beach recorded wind gusts up to 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast toward Victoria Island.  The trailing cold front moved onto the coast, bringing strong southerly winds ahead of the front to the coast and the coast range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clatskanie/Mist Emergency Manager reported several trees down in the area, including along highway 202.  Tidewater RAWS recorded wind gusts up to 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through northwest Oregon the morning of December 11th, lifting snow levels up high enough for precipitation to fall as rain ahead of the cold front that afternoon.  Surface temperatures on Mt Hood, however, were still below freezing so rain froze on contact with surfaces and created a layer of ice. Once the cold front passed, snow levels dropped, changing precipitation back over to snow, and 10 inches of snow fell overnight December 11 to December 12.  Rain falling at lower elevations also caused a landslide in the Columbia Gorge the night of December 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain starting the afternoon of the 11th left a layer of ice on everything, followed by 10 inches of snow overnight into the morning of the 12th.  This caused problems on ski lifts at Mt Hood Meadows the morning of the 12th, closing down ski lifts, and leaving over 150 passengers stranded. All were successfully evacuated.

Surface temperatures on Mt Hood, however, were still below freezing so rain froze on contact with surfaces and created a layer of ice. Rain falling at lower elevations also caused a landslide in the Columbia Gorge the night of December 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain falling on the Eagle Creek burn scar caused a landslide that blocked exit 41 on I-84 for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: We saw post-frontal showers on December 1, associated with a low pressure system right over Oregon.  There was enough directional veering of the winds for a funnel cloud to develop associated with one of the stronger showers over Marion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small funnel cloud was reported southeast of Dundee and a picture was relayed to the National Weather Service by KGW-TV.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL COAST RANGE OF W OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Alaska brought a strong cold front through.  This generated strong winds across northwest Oregon, and also brought heavy rain which caused flooding on the Tillamook river.  Large seas also caused damage in spots along beaches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power outages were reported by PGE.  Peak wind gusts of 35 to 43 mph were recorded across the Portland Metro and north Willamette Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Alaska brought a strong cold front through.  This generated strong winds across northwest Oregon, and also brought heavy rain which caused flooding on the Tillamook river.  Large seas also caused damage in spots along beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilson River near Tillamook crested at 12.5 feet around 10 AM, which is 0.5 foot abouve flood stage. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Alaska brought a strong cold front through.  This generated strong winds across northwest Oregon, and also brought heavy rain which caused flooding on the Tillamook river.  Large seas also caused damage in spots along beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter sent in pictures of damage from two beachfront buildings.  One looks like the beach eroded beneath the decking, which has caused the decking to sag.  The other looks like waves crashed into the bottom floor causing the wall to be pushed inward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Alaska brought a strong cold front through.  This generated strong winds across northwest Oregon, and also brought heavy rain which caused flooding on the Tillamook river.  Large seas also caused damage in spots along beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations along the central Oregon coast reported wind gusts up to 55 to 70 mph. Highest recorded was on the Yaquina Bridge, which recorded wind gusts up to 79 mph.  There were widespread power outages in Depoe Bay and Lincoln City.  ODOT reported trees down on Highway 101, Highway 229, and Route 34 at milepost 5.5.
